planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist to well-drained woodland soil in partial to full shade; eastern woodland sedge spreads slowly by rhizomes to form a low, turf-like groundcover and is much easier to establish from divisions than from seed.

directions: Plant in moist to average, humus-rich, acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; eastern woodland sedge is a low, graceful, soft-textured native sedge of eastern US woodlands that forms a loose, arching groundcover in shaded conditions where grass will not grow. Unlike many sedges, it adapts well to moderately dry conditions once established and tolerates the competition of tree roots under mature oaks and maples. The slender, bright green leaves arch gently to about 12–18 inches, providing a fine, grass-like texture in the shade garden. Excellent as a low-maintenance lawn substitute under trees, or as a no-mow groundcover that requires no fertilizer, irrigation, or edging once established. Seeds are eaten by several sparrow species. Naturally colonizes disturbed woodland edges and is one of the most adaptable native sedges for garden use.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall or early spring in moist, woodland soil in shade; blue wood sedge establishes more readily from fall-planted divisions which give the roots time to establish before summer heat. The blue-green foliage is evergreen in zones 7-9.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; blue wood sedge is prized for its distinctly blue-green to glaucous foliage — a true blue-tinged color unusual among groundcover plants — that remains evergreen in the warm winters of zones 7–9 and provides striking color contrast against darker green ferns, hostas, and woodland perennials. It is a southeastern Coastal Plain and Piedmont native that tolerates the humid heat of zone 8–9 far better than many northern sedges, making it a premium choice for Southern shade gardens. The low, arching clumps spread slowly by rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat. Essentially maintenance-free once established in appropriate moist, shaded soil. The glaucous leaf coloration is most intense in deeper shade.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in well-drained to average, moist soil in shade or partial shade; Texas sedge is adapted to the dry, shaded conditions under live oaks and cedar elms of Texas and is among the most drought-tolerant sedges available for zone 7–9 shade gardens.

directions: Plant in well-drained to average soil in partial to full shade; Texas sedge is one of the most valuable native groundcovers for the difficult dry-shade conditions found under live oaks, cedar elms, and other dense-canopy trees in Texas and the Gulf Coast. The very fine, soft, bright green blades stay only 4–8 inches tall and spread slowly to form a delicate turf that can be mowed occasionally or left unmowed as a naturalistic groundcover. It is notably more drought tolerant than most carex species, surviving the dry summers under mature trees where more moisture-demanding groundcovers wilt and die. Excellent as a lawn alternative in shade where traditional turfgrasses thin out and fail. Seeds are tiny and eaten by many ground-foraging birds. Evergreen in zone 8–10.

planting.note: Plant divisions in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in shade; Cherokee sedge grows naturally in low, moist woodland bottomlands and creek margins of the southeastern Coastal Plain and requires consistent moisture to perform well.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Cherokee sedge is a taller, more vigorous shade sedge native to the moist bottomland forests and creek banks of the southeastern US, from North Carolina west to Texas. The arching, strap-like dark green blades are among the boldest of any native carex species and create a lush, tropical-looking groundcover in moist, shaded bottomland gardens. Unlike many sedges, it tolerates periodic flooding and will grow happily at the wet edge of a pond or stream bank in shade. Particularly valuable for filling wet, low, shaded areas under large trees near water features. Spreads moderately by short rhizomes to form colonies. Provides important habitat structure for ground-nesting birds and small mammals in wet woodland settings.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, rocky, limestone-derived or calcareous soil in full sun; seep muhly is especially adapted to thin, rocky, alkaline soils over limestone outcrops and caliche in central Texas.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, rocky or sandy, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; seep muhly produces an autumn show similar to the more widely planted Gulf muhly (M. capillaris), with long, wispy pink to rose-purple flower plumes in October and November — but on a finer-textured, more drought-tolerant plant better adapted to the alkaline limestone soils of central Texas and the Edwards Plateau. The plumes catch morning light magnificently and the plant is excellent in mass plantings on rocky, dry slopes where Gulf muhly struggles. Unlike Gulf muhly, which prefers more moisture, seep muhly thrives in the xeric, calcareous, rocky soils that define the Hill Country landscape. Named for its natural habitat of seeps and rocky canyon sides, it tolerates both the dry periods between rains and the occasional wet flush. A premium native for zone 7–9 limestone gardens.
